heading: Understanding Limits and One‑Sided Limits
body: A limit describes the behavior of a function as the input approaches a particular point. Formally, \(\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=L\) means that for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists a \(\delta>0\) such that \(|x-c|<\delta\) implies \(|f(x)-L|<\varepsilon\). One‑sided limits restrict \(x\) to approach \(c\) from the left (\(x\to c^-\)) or right (\(x\to c^+\)), allowing us to handle discontinuities and piecewise definitions. These concepts underpin continuity: a function is continuous at \(c\) when \(\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=f(c)\).

title: Techniques for Evaluating Limits
description: Covers algebraic manipulation, rationalizing, squeeze theorem, L'Hôpital's Rule, and the use of series expansions to compute limits that are indeterminate forms.
